title: Covering case boards with leather
subtitle: Paring, gluing, and turning in leather on bookbinding boards
excerpt: Covering boards in leather requires three skills working in sequence: paring the edges thin enough for invisible turn-ins, applying adhesive at the right consistency, and pressing the leather firmly into the hinge groove before the adhesive sets. This tutorial works through the technique on a single board before tackling a full case.
sourceNotes: Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), the forwarding chapter on covering in leather: detailed method for covering a case with a pared skin, managing the turn-ins, and pressing at the groove.

glossaryTerms.definition: Thinning the flesh side of leather with a blade so that the turn-in edges lie flat without producing a visible ridge on the board.
The channel running parallel to the spine on each board, set inward from the spine edge, that allows the case to open and close without cracking the covering material.

content.text: Covering a board in leather is a smaller-scale version of covering a full case. Practising on one board before covering both boards of a book is the sensible approach. The three skills involved in
and covering build on each other: you cannot correct a turn-in that is too thick once the adhesive has set, so each step must be done right before moving to the next.

attrs.heading: Cutting tools safety
attrs.body: Paring knives are extremely sharp. Work on a stable surface with the leather held flat. Never pare toward the body. Change the blade when you feel the tool dragging rather than slicing.

items.name: Vegetable-tanned leather, 1.0 to 1.5 mm
items.notes: A piece 40 mm larger than the board on all four sides, to allow for the turn-in zone.

items.name: PVA, acid-free mixed with wheat paste, 1:1
items.notes: Mixed adhesive gives a longer working time than PVA alone. The paste-heavy mix is more forgiving for repositioning the leather before it tacks.

items.name: Bone folder (standard)
items.notes: For pressing the leather onto the board surface.

items.name: Bone folder (Teflon)
items.notes: For smoothing the turn-ins without shine marks.

content.text: On the flesh side of the leather, mark a line 20 mm from each edge with a blunt point. Working from this line to the edge, pare the leather with the paring knife at a shallow angle, removing material in thin strokes until the edge tapers from full thickness to near-translucent. Test the edge by folding it against a board: a well-pared turn-in bends without creasing. The leather is pared correctly when you can just see light through the thinnest point.
Apply adhesive to leather and board

Apply the PVA-paste mixture to the flesh side of the leather with the paste brush, spreading evenly from the centre outward. Apply a thin coat to the board face as well. Allow 45 to 60 seconds for the adhesive to become slightly tacky before positioning. Lay the leather grain-side up onto the board, pressing from the centre outward to eliminate air bubbles.
Before turning in the edges, press the leather firmly into the

with the rounded end of the bone folder, running from head to tail in one pass. The leather must sit in the groove cleanly; a bridge of leather across the groove makes a stiff, fragile joint. Once the groove is set, rub the whole board surface firmly to press out any remaining air.
Turn in and mitre the corners

Fold the head and tail edges over first, pressing firmly onto the inside face of the board with the Teflon folder. Then fold the spine and foredge edges. At each corner, cut a mitre: remove a 45-degree triangle of leather leaving just enough to meet cleanly at the corner point. Press each corner firmly, making sure the two meeting edges butt without a gap or an overlap.

items.symptom: A ridge is visible on the inside of the board at each turn-in edge
items.cause: The leather was not pared thinly enough at the turn-in zone.
items.fix: Pare more aggressively on the next attempt. On a finished board, a layer of endpaper glued over the interior can reduce the ridge visually.

items.cause: One side of the leather dried faster than the other, or the board was wet unevenly.
items.fix: Press under weight until fully dry. Pre-dampen the grain side of the leather lightly before applying adhesive to both sides; it dries more evenly.

items.symptom: The corner mitre has a gap
items.cause: The triangle cut was too deep, removing material that should bridge the corner.
items.fix: Leave more material at the corner on the next cut. The correct mitre removes a triangle whose point is 2 mm from the corner of the board.
